threats.title: Governance Bypass: Defeated and Expired Proposals Can Be Executed
threats.hypothesis_description: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() functions allow execution of proposals in Defeated, Expired, and Succeeded states. An attacker can create a malicious governance proposal containing arbitrary external calls, have it be voted down by the community, and still execute the malicious payload by calling execute().
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement explicitly allows Defeated and Expired states: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)`. This completely nullifies the governance voting mechanism and allows any proposer to execute arbitrary actions through the governor contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require allows ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired in addition to Succeeded

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to meet proposalThreshold (default 0, so any holder)
Governor contract has been deployed and holds assets or controls privileged operations
Voting period completes (proposal reaches Defeated or Expired state)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: execute() requires status == Succeeded || Defeated || Expired instead of just Succeeded
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit proposal with arbitrary calldata targeting governor-controlled assets, let it be defeated, call execute()
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All assets controlled by governance are at risk - arbitrary contract calls are executed via _execute loop

economic_questions: What assets does the governor control or can relay to?
What is the proposalThreshold - can any token holder submit proposals?
Are there timelocks or additional guards on execution?
validation_requirements: Verify both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() have the same bug
Confirm state() function returns Defeated/Expired correctly
Test end-to-end: propose, defeat, execute malicious payload

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for propose() + execute() calls (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total value controlled by governance
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI if governance controls significant assets

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after voting period ends

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and governance participants
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets accessible via governor execute/relay functions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify state machine transitions for Defeated proposals

threats.title: Unvalidated Genesis Pool Address Allows Gauge Creation for Arbitrary Pairs
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() does not validate that the genesisPool address parameter was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken(), triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a real gauge for an attacker-controlled pair address and whitelists an arbitrary token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function accepts any non-zero address as genesisPool. A malicious contract can return controlled values from getGenesisInfo() and getLiquidityPoolInfo(), causing gaugeManager.createGauge() to be called with an arbitrary pair address and tokenHandler.whitelistToken() with an arbitrary token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No factory validation on genesisPool parameter; any address accepted if non-zero

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
gaugeManager.createGauge() does not independently validate the pair was created by pairFactory
No additional access control on depositToken beyond nonReentrant

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy FakeGenesisPool returning true from depositToken and attacker-controlled pairAddress from getLiquidityPoolInfo
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Redirect gauge emissions to attacker-controlled pair; whitelist arbitrary tokens for trading

economic_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge validate the pool address against the factory?
What emissions can be redirected to a fraudulently created gauge?
Can arbitrary token whitelisting be weaponized for price manipulation?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock FakeGenesisPool and call depositToken through manager
Verify gauge creation succeeds for arbitrary pair address
Quantify emissions redirectable to fraudulent gauge

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying fake contract + depositToken call (~0.05 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Gauge emissions per epoch directed to attacker's pair
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high if emissions are significant
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to create gauge; ongoing emission theft

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes legitimate gauge emissions; whitelists potentially malicious tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate gauge voters and LPs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced emissions proportional to attacker's fraudulent gauge weight

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify fake pool can trigger full _preLaunchPool flow

threats.title: setRouter() Inverted Zero-Address Check Permanently Bricks Router Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter() contains require(_router == address(0)) instead of require(_router != address(0)). This means the only value that can be set is address(0), making it impossible to update the router to a valid address. If called with address(0), all subsequent launch() calls will revert because addLiquidity is called on address(0).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code typo/defect. The pattern `require(x == address(0), 'ZA')` is the opposite of every other zero-address validation in the codebase which uses `require(x != address(0), 'ZA')`. If the owner ever calls setRouter(address(0)), all genesis pool launches become permanently impossible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_router == address(0), 'ZA') should be require(_router != address(0), 'ZA')

preconditions: Owner calls setRouter with address(0) (only accepted value)
Or owner attempts to update router to valid address (will always revert)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(_router == address(0)) accepts only zero address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner cannot update router; if called with 0, router is bricked permanently
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All genesis pool launches freeze - native and funding tokens locked in pools

economic_questions: Is the current router address set during initialize() still valid?
Can router be set through other paths (e.g., proxy upgrade)?
How much value would be locked if launches are bricked?
validation_requirements: Confirm setRouter reverts for any non-zero address
Verify no alternative path to update router exists
Test impact on launch() when router is address(0)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - code defect triggered by normal admin operation
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - denial of service, not direct profit

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Triggered when owner attempts router update
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All pending genesis pool launches blocked indefinitely

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All genesis pool depositors and token owners
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Deposits potentially locked until workaround found
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending genesis pool deposits and native token allocations

threats.title: Zero Slippage Protection in addLiquidity Enables Value Extraction During Launch
threats.hypothesis_description: _addLiquidityAndDistribute passes 0 for both amountAMin and amountBMin when calling IRouter.addLiquidity(). Since pair creation is observable on-chain after approveGenesisPool, an attacker can manipulate the pair's reserves before the launch transaction by directly transferring tokens to the pair address. The zero slippage tolerance means addLiquidity accepts any ratio.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pair is created during approveGenesisPool and its address is publicly known. Between approval and launch (potentially multiple epochs), an attacker can deposit tokens into the pair to skew the ratio. When launch() executes with 0 min amounts, value is extracted from genesis pool depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IRouter(_router).addLiquidity(..., 0, 0, address(this), block.timestamp + 100) passes 0 for amountAMin and amountBMin

preconditions: Genesis pool has been approved (pair address is known on-chain)
Attacker can transfer tokens to the pair and sync() is callable despite genesis status
Launch transaction has not yet been executed

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deposit skewed token amounts to pair before launch, then arbitrage after
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = value extracted from skewed liquidity ratio - cost of manipulation

economic_questions: Does pairFactory.setGenesisStatus block sync() and direct token transfers?
What is the time window between approval and launch?
What percentage of liquidity value can be extracted?
validation_requirements: Test if tokens can be sent to the pair and reserves manipulated during genesis status
Calculate maximum extractable value given typical pool sizes
Verify router behavior with pre-existing pair reserves

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to genesis pool size for meaningful manipulation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for transfer + sync + post-launch arbitrage
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to significant percentage of total liquidity value
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on pool size and manipulation capital ratio
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block: manipulate reserves then wait for launch

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After pool approval, before epoch flip launch
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Genesis pool depositors receive less LP value than their deposits warrant

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All genesis pool depositors and token owner
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total genesis pool allocation (native + funding tokens)

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Router addLiquidity behavior with pre-existing reserves

threats.title: Abstain Votes Weaponized to Force Proposals into Expired State for Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorCountingSimple, _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes AND forVotes > abstainVotes. An attacker can cast abstain votes to ensure neither _voteSucceeded nor _voteDefeated returns true, pushing the proposal into Expired state. Combined with ECON-H001, the Expired proposal can still be executed via execute().
threats.investigation_rationale: The voting logic creates a third outcome (Expired) where neither success nor defeat is determined. Since execute() allows Expired state, an attacker can strategically use abstain votes to ensure their own proposal lands in Expired, guaranteeing execution regardless of community opposition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes; _voteDefeated requires againstVotes > forVotes && againstVotes > abstainVotes

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient smNFT voting power to make abstainVotes > forVotes
Governor.sol variant (smNFT-based) is deployed and in use
Quorum is reached (forVotes + abstainVotes >= quorum)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _voteSucceeded: forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes; neither success nor defeat -> Expired -> executable
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit malicious proposal, cast large abstain votes so abstainVotes > forVotes, ensuring Expired state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Proposal executes despite majority opposition, bypassing governance

economic_questions: How much voting power is needed to force Expired state?
Can a single large smNFT holder force any proposal into Expired?
What is the cost of acquiring sufficient voting power?
validation_requirements: Verify state machine: quorum reached + neither succeeded nor defeated = Expired
Confirm Expired state is executable via execute()
Calculate minimum abstain votes needed given typical voter participation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to acquire smNFTs with sufficient voting power

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Quorum numerator, typical voter participation rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: State transition: quorum met + !succeeded + !defeated = Expired = executable

threats.title: Quorum Manipulation via Non-Snapshot Total Supply in Governor.sol
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction, the quorum() function calls token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp parameter, calculating quorum against current total supply rather than the snapshot supply at proposal creation. This allows quorum manipulation by changing total supply between proposal creation and voting deadline.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function signature accepts blockTimestamp but the implementation ignores it: `return (token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator()`. This means quorum is recalculated every time state() is called, using the live total supply.

proof_of_signal.pattern: quorum() ignores blockTimestamp parameter, calls getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without timestamp argument

preconditions: Attacker can influence smNFT total supply (minting/burning mechanisms exist)
Governor.sol smNFT-based variant is deployed
getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() returns current supply without timestamp filter

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Decrease total supply after proposal creation to lower quorum, enabling weak proposals to pass
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Pass malicious proposals with minority support by manipulating quorum threshold

economic_questions: What mechanisms exist to change smNFT total supply?
How much can total supply change during a voting period?
What is the cost to meaningfully manipulate total supply?
validation_requirements: Verify getsmNFTPastTotalSupply returns current vs historical supply
Calculate supply change needed to flip quorum outcome
Test quorum recalculation during active proposal

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to smNFT supply manipulation required
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of governance actions that pass with lowered quorum
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Within voting period of target proposal

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Governance decisions made without proper quorum
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Assets controlled via governance with manipulated quorum

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: quorumNumerator value, current smNFT total supply
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm quorum uses live supply not snapshot

threats.title: Single Broken Genesis Pool DoS Blocks All Epoch Flip Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens and make external calls for each pool. If any single pool's external call reverts (e.g., a malicious token that reverts on transfer, or a broken gauge), the entire transaction reverts, blocking all other pools from transitioning. The pre_epoch_period update in checkBeforeEpochFlip only occurs after all iterations, so it remains stale on revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: No try-catch wraps the per-pool operations. A single reverted call to pairFactory.setGenesisStatus, IGenesisPool.setPoolStatus, or addLiquidity blocks the entire batch. This creates a permanent DoS where the failing pool prevents all other pools from launching or transitioning.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop over liveNativeTokens with external calls and no try-catch; single revert blocks entire function

preconditions: At least one genesis pool in liveNativeTokens has a token or external dependency that reverts
epochController calls checkAtEpochFlip or checkBeforeEpochFlip
The reverting pool cannot be manually removed before the next epoch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No try-catch around per-pool external calls in batch processing loop
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create a genesis pool with a token whose transfer reverts under certain conditions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Block all competing pool launches to gain market advantage or grief depositors

economic_questions: Can governance manually remove the problematic pool to unblock others?
What types of token behavior trigger reverts in the launch flow?
How long can the DoS persist before recovery?
validation_requirements: Create a genesis pool with a reverting token and verify it blocks epoch flip
Test if rejectGenesisPool can remove the offending pool
Verify pre_epoch_period remains stale after failed checkBeforeEpochFlip

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating a genesis pool with a malicious token + whitelist requirement
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect - blocks competing launches; depositor funds stuck
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Griefing attack - no direct profit but significant harm

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal - just enough to create genesis pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before epoch flip when multiple pools are pending
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All pending genesis pool launches indefinitely delayed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors in pending genesis pools
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all deposits across all pending genesis pools

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of live pools, recovery mechanisms available
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no try-catch and revert propagation

threats.title: Incentive Tokens Irrecoverable After TransferIncentives When Pool Transitions to NOT_QUALIFIED
threats.hypothesis_description: After transferIncentives() sends incentive tokens to the external bribe contract, the incentives mapping is not zeroed. If the pool subsequently transitions to NOT_QUALIFIED via setPoolStatus(), claimIncentives() will attempt to transfer tokens the pool no longer holds, permanently reverting. The token owner sees claimable amounts but can never recover them.
threats.investigation_rationale: transferIncentives iterates over incentiveTokens and sends each to external bribe via notifyRewardAmount, but never sets incentives[token] = 0. The _setPoolStatus allows any transition via manager. If pool reaches PRE_LAUNCH (incentives already sent) and then is set to NOT_QUALIFIED, claimIncentives tries to transfer tokens with zero balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferIncentives sends tokens to external_bribe but does not zero incentives[token]; claimIncentives at line 220-230 reads non-zero values and attempts transfer

preconditions: Token owner has added incentives before PRE_LAUNCH
transferIncentives has been called (pool transitioned to PRE_LAUNCH)
Manager sets pool status to NOT_QUALIFIED after incentives were transferred

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: incentives[token] remains non-zero after transferIncentives sends tokens away
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: State transition path: incentives added → transferred → pool rejected → claim fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token owner loses all incentive tokens permanently (no recovery mechanism)

economic_questions: What is the typical value of incentives per genesis pool?
Can the external bribe contract return incentive tokens?
Is there an admin recovery mechanism?
validation_requirements: Verify incentives mapping is not zeroed in transferIncentives
Confirm claimIncentives reverts when pool balance is zero
Test state transition path PRE_LAUNCH -> NOT_QUALIFIED

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - bug triggered by state transition
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - fund loss, not direct theft

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When governance rejects a pool after incentive transfer
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Token owner loses incentive tokens permanently

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all incentive tokens in affected pools

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify incentives not zeroed after transfer

threats.title: deductAmount Arithmetic Underflow Can Permanently Lock Token Owner LP Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In deductAmount(), when the token owner unstakes an amount that crosses the boundary between their owner portion and depositor portion, integer rounding in the subtraction `userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity` can produce a value larger than userDeposits[account], causing an underflow revert at `userDeposits[account] -= userAmount`.
threats.investigation_rationale: The division truncation in `(totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity` can round down, making the subtracted portion smaller than expected, leaving userAmount too large. With specific liquidity and deposit values, this creates an irrecoverable state where the token owner cannot unstake.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userAmount = (totalDeposits * gaugeTokenAmount) / _depositerLiquidity followed by userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity; rounding difference can cause userDeposits[account] underflow

preconditions: Token owner is also a depositor (has non-zero userDeposits)
Liquidity, totalDeposits, and gaugeTokenAmount produce rounding discrepancy
Token owner attempts partial unstake crossing owner/depositor boundary

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Two separate integer divisions on numerators that should cancel but don't due to truncation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Specific values of liquidity, totalDeposits, and gaugeTokenAmount trigger underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token owner's LP permanently locked - no direct profit but permanent fund loss

economic_questions: What combinations of liquidity and deposit values trigger the underflow?
Can the token owner avoid this by unstaking in different amounts?
Is deductAllAmount a safe alternative?
validation_requirements: Compute specific input values that trigger the underflow
Test with fuzzing: various liquidity, totalDeposits, gaugeTokenAmount combinations
Verify Solidity 0.8.13 overflow protection catches this as revert

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent lock of affected LP tokens

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When token owner tries to unstake
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Token owner LP permanently locked in gauge

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Token owner who is also a depositor
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Token owner's share of genesis pool liquidity

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Find concrete (liquidity, totalDeposits, gaugeTokenAmount) triple that triggers underflow

threats.title: balanceOf Division by Zero When totalDeposits Becomes Zero After Launch
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.balanceOf() computes `(_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits`. After launch, if deductAllAmount is called for all depositors (reducing totalDeposits to 0 via deductAmount/deductAllAmount), any subsequent balanceOf query for non-owner accounts divides by zero, reverting. This can break gauge integrations that query balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: deductAllAmount sets `userDeposits[account] = 0` but does not update totalDeposits. deductAmount does subtract from userDeposits but totalDeposits is never decremented in either function. However, after all depositors have deducted their full amounts, totalDeposits still reflects original value. The issue arises if userDeposits for all accounts reach 0 while totalDeposits remains positive, causing a logic inconsistency but not div-by-zero. But if totalDeposits were ever set to 0 through another path, div-by-zero occurs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balance = (_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits with no zero-check on totalDeposits

preconditions: Pool has been launched (liquidity > 0)
totalDeposits could theoretically become 0 (edge case in accounting)
External contract queries balanceOf after accounting edge case

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Division by totalDeposits without zero-check in balanceOf
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Accounting edge case where totalDeposits reaches 0 while liquidity > 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS on gauge/integration contracts that query balanceOf

economic_questions: Can totalDeposits actually reach 0 in any valid execution path?
What gauge functions depend on balanceOf?
Does the revert cascade to block other critical operations?
validation_requirements: Trace all paths that modify totalDeposits
Verify deductAmount/deductAllAmount affect totalDeposits correctly
Test balanceOf with edge case deposit values

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks gauge integration with genesis pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Gauge and integration contracts querying balanceOf
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Operational integrity of gauge system for this pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine if totalDeposits can reach 0 in practice
